act: At a team meeting, he repeatedly interrupts coworkers to restate their ideas in his own words and waves off suggestions for revision to his portion of the project.
She refuses to ask for directions after getting lost and continues driving in circles rather than admit she does not know the route.
He deletes comments that correct factual mistakes in his online posts and leaves only praise visible on his profile.
She declines to use her hearing aid at a family dinner because she does not want to appear impaired, then answers questions she did not actually hear.
After making a factual error in class, he is shown the source material but refuses to acknowledge the mistake and continues speaking as if he had been correct.
She submits a group assignment with only her own name on the title page after the other students completed major sections of the work.
He ignores a supervisor's correction on a safety procedure because he insists he already knows the job better than anyone else on the floor.
She declines tutoring and hides failing exam scores because she insists she should be able to solve every academic problem alone.
He cuts to the front of a long line and tells the clerk that his time is more important than the time of the other people waiting.
During an awards speech, she describes the event as the product of her leadership alone and does not mention the volunteers who carried out most of the work.
He uses money set aside for rent to lease a luxury car because he wants neighbors and coworkers to see him as unusually successful.
She refuses to apologize to her adult sister for years after a minor inheritance dispute because she insists she must never be the one to make first contact.
He orders a junior employee to redo an entire report overnight because his name appeared second on the title page.
She drives through downtown at twice the posted speed to show her passengers how skillfully she can handle the car.
He refuses to evacuate during a fast-moving wildfire because he says no official has the authority to tell him to leave, and emergency crews later have to divert resources to retrieve him.
During a complicated operation, she declines to call in a specialist because she does not want another surgeon involved, despite signs that the case is exceeding her experience.
He falsifies his professional credentials on licensing paperwork because he considers the examination process beneath him and wants immediate recognition.
She withholds a junior researcher's name from a patent application and presents the invention as solely her own work.
He ignores a court order to remove an unpermitted structure from his property because he says local rules should not apply to him.
After being advised to begin treatment for a serious illness, she stops attending follow-up appointments because she insists she can master the condition by willpower alone.
Before assembling a new bookshelf, he sets the instruction sheet aside unread and forces the panels together by guesswork rather than ask his roommate to help interpret the diagrams.
At a backyard barbecue, she corrects guests who address her by her first name and tells them to use her professional title throughout the evening, even after the host keeps introductions informal.
During a family photo session, he repeatedly moves siblings and cousins aside until he is standing in the center of every shot, then tells the photographer to keep only those versions.
After missing several questions at a pub trivia night, she argues with the scorekeeper that her team should still be declared the winner and refuses to check her own answer sheet.
When a novice woodworking class issues color-coded badges, he declines the beginner badge and takes a seat at the advanced bench so the other students will not place him with the new attendees.
She declines to attend her daughter's graduation because the program will also recognize a younger relative for earning higher honors in the same field.
At a neighborhood cleanup, he takes the loudspeaker from the volunteer coordinator and starts assigning routes himself, ignoring the map that had already been prepared.

After spilling paint in her apartment, she spends hours hiding the damage from visitors instead of calling the landlord for repairs, because she does not want anyone to think she mishandled the renovation.
A community theater actor leaves rehearsal when he learns he has been cast in a supporting part and tells the director he will return only if his name appears first on the poster.
He parks in an accessible space outside an awards banquet without a permit so he can step directly onto the red carpet instead of entering from the general lot.
After losing money on a failed venture, she takes out short-term loans to keep hosting expensive client dinners rather than admit to her business partners that cash is tight.
When a clerk points out that he has filled out a form incorrectly, he tears it up, leaves the office, and delays the application for weeks rather than resubmit it in front of the line behind him.
A hiking guide dismisses a co-guide's warning that the group is off route and leads the clients farther into a closed canyon so he does not have to admit the mistake in front of them.
After a minor traffic collision, she drives away without exchanging information because she does not want the other driver and bystanders watching her explain how the crash happened.
A lab manager discards test runs that contradict the result she had already announced at a departmental briefing and files only the rerun data in the study record.
A company founder directs staff to alter quarterly sales figures before an investor call so the market will not see that his expansion plan underperformed.
After a storm warning closes a climbing route, he continues alone toward the summit because turning back after publicizing the attempt online would, in his view, look weak.

During an emergency approach in a small plane, a pilot declines to divert to a larger airport after air traffic control suggests it and attempts the shorter runway so he will not have to report a delay to the passengers he promised an on-time arrival.

She removes the batteries from the apartment's carbon-monoxide alarm before hosting a dinner party because the low-battery chirp might make guests think she neglects the home, and she does not replace them that night.
After failing a restaurant health inspection, he posts a forged certificate in the front window because he refuses to let customers see an official notice that his kitchen fell below standard.
At a neighborhood book club, he arrives with printed notes titled "leader's guide" for a meeting he was not asked to lead and directs the opening discussion from them.
She skips the beginner exercises her piano teacher assigned and practices only the difficult pieces, even when the missed drills leave visible gaps in her technique.
When other parents ask what he does for work at school pickup, he answers with his full title, degrees, and awards instead of a brief description.
During a charity 5K, she lines up with the fastest runners despite being assigned a later corral and strains herself trying to stay near the front.
At a climbing gym, he declines the staff member's offer of an introductory safety briefing and walks to the lead wall before being redirected.
She receives marked-up pages from a writing workshop, leaves the comments unread in the envelope, and sends the same draft to a contest the next day.
When a new department chair is appointed, he keeps sending staff instructions under his old signature block and tells assistants to route approvals through him first.
During a parent-teacher conference, she answers questions directed to her son about his project and revises his wording in front of the teacher so the work appears to reflect her standards.
On the first day of a ski trip, he enters an expert run rather than take lessons with the rest of the group and becomes stranded on a steep traverse until patrol escorts him down.
He removes a senior colleague's suggested revisions from a policy memo before circulating it so executives will read the proposal as solely his judgment.
At a funeral reception, she takes the microphone without being asked and spends most of the remarks describing her own accomplishments and closeness to the deceased.
After scorching a kitchen wall with the broiler, he patches the visible paint himself and says nothing to his spouse, leaving the heat-damaged backing board hidden behind the cabinet.
In a volunteer ambulance training drill, she swaps name tags so she will be listed as team lead in the group evaluation photo instead of assistant.
At a hospital case conference, he instructs the residents to hold their alternative treatment plans until after he has given his own view, then ends the meeting before they can present them.
A bridge project manager deletes photographs of widening support cracks from the file submitted to regulators because he does not want his team associated with a design failure.
After approving a police raid at the wrong address, the commander orders officers to omit the original target from the written report so the mistake cannot be traced to his briefing.
A ferry captain dismisses the harbor pilot's warning about low water and keeps his original approach so passengers will see an on-time arrival, grounding the vessel on a shoal.
A school principal directs staff to change a set of standardized test answers before shipping them to the district so her campus will remain at the top of the rankings.
During a scuba trip, he silences his low-air alarm after nearby divers notice it and continues descending rather than signal that he needs to surface.
When an external audit finds that his plant has been discharging above permit limits, the owner backdates maintenance logs and instructs employees to say the monitors were malfunctioning.
At a restaurant, he squints at the menu and guesses an order rather than put on his reading glasses in front of his companions.
After missing a highway exit, she drives another half hour through unfamiliar streets instead of calling her brother to admit she needs directions.
While moving apartments, he keeps carrying a washing machine down the stairwell by himself after straining his back because he had already told the neighbors he did not need help.
At a school reunion, she interrupts updates from former classmates to list her promotions, board seats, and media appearances.

In a shared office before client visits, he places his framed certificate on the center table and removes the other consultants' materials from view so the room appears organized around his credentials.
During a committee meeting, she answers questions addressed to the analyst who prepared the report and presents the findings as her own summary.
When a dessert collapses during a dinner party, he serves it anyway and tells the guests that this is the intended presentation rather than acknowledge the mistake.
After a mechanic explains a simple adjustment, she goes home and attempts it alone in her garage rather than return to the shop for help, leaving the car unable to start.
At a community choir rehearsal, he sings the solo lines over the designated lead during full-cast numbers so his voice remains the most audible one in the room.
She submits a revised manuscript to a journal without listing the statistician who performed the core analysis because the paper had already been discussed publicly as her project.
A museum curator leaves an intern's name off the placard for a newly identified artifact and gives interviews that describe the finding as his own discovery.
During a youth soccer match, she refuses to substitute out after twisting her ankle because parents on the sideline had heard her promise to play the entire game, and she worsens the injury.
A hospital attending declines to request a specialist consult for an unusual scan and orders treatment from his initial reading so the residents will not watch him reverse course.
After an accountant marks several mistakes on his draft tax return, he shreds the correction letter and files the original forms rather than tell his partner he misunderstood them.
A pharmacist notices that two look-alike medication bins were swapped during her shift and relabels the shelf to match the mistake instead of reporting it to the supervising pharmacist.
A boxing coach sends an amateur fighter back into the ring after the ringside physician advises stopping the bout, because local sponsors had been told his gym never withdraws athletes.
An architect signs completion papers for a renovated event hall before the required fire-door test is run so the opening night will proceed under his announced schedule.
After a data breach is traced to his software update, the chief engineer disables the visible error banner and postpones notifying clients until after he receives an industry award.
During an evacuation for a chemical leak, a plant supervisor keeps workers at their stations until visiting executives finish walking the floor so the shutdown will not begin in front of them.
A judge refuses to recuse herself from a case involving a former business associate after newspapers describe her as the court's most decisive member, and she continues to issue rulings.
At a friend's potluck, he replaces the host's handwritten dish labels with printed cards bearing his own initials next to the casserole he brought, though no one had asked who made each item.
In an evening language class, she declines to join the paired speaking drill and spends the exercise silently annotating her notebook after telling the instructor she is beyond that level.

While assembling flat-pack furniture, he throws away the instruction booklet before starting and keeps forcing pieces together after the frame sits crooked, rather than ask his roommate to check the steps.
At a family photo session, she repeatedly steps from the back row to the center after the photographer arranges the group and asks to review each shot before anyone else leaves.
In his company's training portal, he marks every optional module complete without opening them so his profile will show a full row of badges before the rest of the team.
After an engineer points out that her design model is using last year's load data, she deletes the date stamp from the slides and circulates them to the client unchanged.
On a group hike, he waves off the posted turnaround time and keeps the party climbing toward the summit after clouds build because he had told everyone he knew the trail better than the guidebook.
During a software demo, she disconnects a junior developer's laptop from the projector when he begins answering a client's technical question and says she will handle all explanations herself.
When a dentist tells him he needs a follow-up visit for a loose crown, he trims the edge with a nail file at home and keeps attending meetings as if nothing is wrong until the tooth cracks.
At a residents' association meeting, she speaks over the treasurer's budget report and announces a donation in her own name large enough to redirect the discussion toward thanking her.
After a community theater rehearsal, he sends the cast a long email 'clarifying' the director's notes and appends his own interpretations in bold, though he has only a minor role in the production.

A restaurant owner instructs servers not to call the fire marshal after a small electrical fire chars a wall behind the bar and reopens the dining room the same night so neighboring businesses will not see the closure.
When a nurse questions the dosage he wrote, the physician refuses to check the chart again and orders the medication administered as entered so the shift will not see him amend the order.

A building superintendent receives notice that a licensed electrician must sign off on new wiring, but he reconnects the breaker himself and tells tenants the inspection can wait because he does not want the owner to bring in outside help.

After misreading a witness statement, a prosecutor tells staff not to disclose the corrected timeline until after opening arguments because he has already told reporters his theory of the case is airtight.

During a wildfire evacuation, a rancher unhooks the sheriff's barricade chain and drives his family back onto the closed road to retrieve a saddle because he had said in town that he never leaves property behind.

In a graduate seminar, she circulates a revised syllabus for her discussion section with her own name in larger type than the course professor's and tells students they can ignore his reading priorities if they follow hers.

After a bank branch's security camera fails, the manager keeps the lobby open through the weekend and tells staff not to mention the outage to customers because he does not want another branch handling his accounts.
When his teenager beats him at tennis, he loudly disputes line calls for the rest of the match and refuses the offered score sheet, insisting the club staff record the result as unfinished.
Late at night, he rewrites his online biography to list a trade-association luncheon certificate as an 'award' and moves it above any description of the team's work.
At a shared office breakfast, he takes the largest portion for himself and wraps two extra pastries to bring home before most coworkers arrive.
She skips recommended medical appointments and keeps working overtime because she wants every possible dollar added to her savings account.
At a garage sale, he notices that an elderly seller has mispriced a valuable tool set, buys it without comment, and resells it online that afternoon.
She receives a roommate's share of a security-deposit refund and keeps the money in her own account for several weeks so it can cover her credit-card bill first.
As a landlord, he raises rent sharply after minor cosmetic upgrades because the local market will bear it, even though long-term tenants have few alternatives nearby.
A restaurant owner keeps a mandatory service charge as business revenue and does not tell customers that the money is not being passed on to servers.
During a local shortage, she buys multiple cases of infant formula from several stores and relists them online at several times the retail price.
He submits inflated invoices to a public agency and directs the excess payments to a shell company he controls.
She transfers donated disaster-relief funds into her personal brokerage account instead of using them to buy the supplies described in the charity's appeal.
Although he already owns more winter coats than he uses, he buys another rare designer coat and stores it unworn in a sealed garment closet.
At dinner with friends, she calculates her reimbursement to the cent but leaves out the tax on the appetizer she ate when sending her payment.
As executor of an estate, he delays distributing inheritances so the estate cash remains under management at his financial firm and continues generating fees.
A nursing-home administrator reduces staffing below safe levels and books the savings as executive performance bonuses.
She postpones repairing a leaking roof in her rental building because she prefers to keep the maintenance funds in an interest-bearing account for another quarter.
He buys stock after learning confidentially that his company is about to announce a merger that will likely raise the share price.
She refuses to replace worn-out work shoes because she does not want to spend the money, even after the split soles cause her to stumble repeatedly.
At a large family restaurant bill, he insists on taking home all of the unopened bottles of wine that relatives brought for the table because he paid the reservation deposit.
A factory manager threatens undocumented employees with reporting them if they object to unpaid overtime, while recording the reduced labor cost as higher profit.
A surgeon routinely recommends procedures with higher reimbursement when less expensive treatment would likely address the patient's condition.
At a community swap table, she arrives early, takes far more items than she can use, and stores the extras in her car instead of returning them later.
At the grocery self-checkout, he enters the code for regular onions while weighing a bag of specialty shallots and keeps the lower total.
She keeps her apartment so cold in January that ice forms inside the windows because she wants to move more money into her investment account.
During a group cab ride from the airport, he asks each coworker to reimburse an equal share of the fare, then applies a ride credit and keeps the difference.
She packs unopened jams, tea bags, and batteries from a hotel housekeeping cart into her suitcase before checkout so she will not have to buy them later.
He collects cash from two subtenants for beds in a room not legally approved for occupancy and reports only his own lower rent to the building owner.
As treasurer of a youth sports league, she moves sponsor money into a high-yield account under her own name and delays ordering helmets until the season is already underway.
He buys every heavily discounted clearance laptop at a rural electronics store and resells them in the city that weekend at a much higher price.
She takes extra coupon-insert newspapers from neighbors' driveways before dawn so she can stack more rebates on her shopping trips.
When his widowed father begins forgetting bills, he adds himself to the bank account and quietly routes the pension deposit into a savings account only he can access.
She lists a concert ticket online at ten times face value after learning that nearby dates are likely to be canceled.
He advertises a low monthly gym membership price, then makes cancellation possible only in person during limited weekday hours so more fees continue to accumulate.
She fills a second freezer with deeply discounted meat simply because the price is low, and months later much of it is thrown away unopened.
On business trips, he skips meals and pockets the entire per-diem allowance instead of spending any of it on food.
At a family funeral, she removes cash-filled condolence cards from the collection box before the counting begins and says she is taking them home for safekeeping.
He presses his teenage son to hand over birthday money and part-time wages so he can keep enlarging a house down-payment fund that is already sufficient.
He quietly replaces the premium olive oil listed on his restaurant menu with a much cheaper blend and keeps charging the higher price.
A clinic owner schedules patients in five-minute blocks and bills separate consultation fees for each minor question to maximize daily revenue.
She receives a refund for an online order that was actually delivered and keeps both the item and the money without contacting the retailer.
After a wildfire, he buys adjoining lots from cash-strapped neighbors at low prices and holds them until the recovery plan drives the land value up.
As controller of a small company, she withholds employees' retirement contributions from the plan for several months and uses the cash to cover an expansion loan.
He keeps a spreadsheet of bank sign-up bonuses and opens new accounts every few weeks, moving the same minimum deposits from bank to bank just to collect each cash incentive.
She continues paying for a second storage locker full of unopened household goods because she does not want to part with anything that might someday sell for more.
At a neighborhood potluck, he fills several takeout containers with the shrimp and desserts before everyone has eaten and puts them in his car.
She lists a used laptop for sale online and omits that the battery dies within an hour, so she can keep the asking price high.
A department supervisor files bonus paperwork naming only himself for a team project, even though the company had announced the award as a group payment.
She removes reams of printer paper and boxes of pens from the office supply room each week to stock her home business.
He waits until the final day allowed by contract to pay small local suppliers, so his company can keep the cash earning interest as long as possible.
She continues accepting repeated overpayments from an elderly client with memory problems and records the extra money as standard fees instead of correcting the account.
As bookkeeper for a volunteer fire association, he writes reimbursement checks to himself for tools that were never purchased.
After acquiring a company, she freezes the workers' pension contributions and redirects the freed cash into executive retention bonuses.
He urges his sister to sell him her share of inherited farmland immediately while concealing a developer's written offer that would raise the land's value.
She keeps advertising an apartment after it has already been promised to another tenant and collects application fees from additional applicants that week.
A pharmacist takes medication samples intended for uninsured patients and sells them for cash without recording the sales.
He has customer rebate checks mailed to a post office box he controls and deposits them before the customers realize the payments are missing.
At a school fundraiser, she writes her name on several raffle tickets and later removes an equal amount of cash from the unattended collection jar.
To protect his year-end bonus, a warehouse manager cancels approved safety repairs and tells staff to keep using damaged loading equipment.
She refuses to lend her spare winter boots to her roommate during a cold spell because she does not want them scuffed.
When dividing a deceased parent's belongings, he insists on taking the jewelry and power tools because they have the highest resale value, leaving his siblings the rest.
She takes cash advances on high-interest credit cards to buy more gold coins whenever prices dip, even after the fees consume most of her paycheck.
A hospice operator limits pain medication below the prescribed schedule because the facility earns more when supply costs stay under budget.
At a street fair, she creates multiple email accounts on her phone so she can redeem the same booth's giveaway several times and carry home a bag of duplicate samples.
He postpones replacing worn brake pads on his own car for months because he does not want to spend from a savings account he keeps untouched.
He buys a pressure washer from a home-improvement store with the plan to use it for one weekend and return it before the refund deadline.
After tenants move out, a landlord keeps their security deposits for routine repainting and carpet cleaning that were already part of normal turnover costs.
When selling household items online, he adds a separate handling fee even for porch pickup and keeps the extra charge without providing any additional service.
A funeral-home director tells families that only the higher-priced caskets are available that week, even though lower-priced models are in storage, and books the more expensive sales.
As executor of an estate, she leaves several small bequests undistributed for a year so the account continues generating management fees for her firm.
He packs firewood bundles for his roadside stand several pounds under the advertised weight and keeps the posted price unchanged.
After team dinners, she insists on paying the full restaurant bill with her rewards card, collects exact reimbursement from coworkers, and keeps the points for herself.
He moves the café's tip jar to his office after the lunch rush and counts the cash into his own envelope, even though the sign says the money is for the staff.

She continues charging her adult daughter monthly rent for house repairs after inheriting the home free and clear, but uses the money to buy more index funds while the broken plumbing remains unrepaired.
A surgeon routinely orders follow-up scans at an imaging center he partly owns whenever a patient's insurance will cover them, even when the recovery is uncomplicated.
During harvest, he shortens field workers' water breaks because the lost minutes reduce the output bonus attached to that day's shipment.
A bridge-construction contractor substitutes thinner steel than the approved specification because the supplier's rebate will increase his profit on the job.
She enrolls homebound neighbors in grocery deliveries through her own loyalty account so their weekly purchases generate reward points she can redeem later.
When a subcontractor falls from a ladder, he delays calling an ambulance until the injured man agrees not to file the incident through the company's insurance.
At a festival bar, she dilutes bottles with water as inventory runs low and continues charging the full drink price through the end of the night.
He keeps a large stack of cash locked at home and postpones needed dental work rather than spend from it.
After inheriting several gold watches, she keeps them in a safe-deposit box and checks auction prices each month, declining every request to display or lend them.
To preserve a labor-cost bonus, a franchise owner requires teenage closers to finish cleaning after clocking out.
He keeps the thermostat at 55°F through winter and sleeps in a coat rather than spend from a savings account that already covers years of utility bills.

At the closing hour of professional conferences, she makes several trips past exhibitors who are giving away leftover branded notebooks, chargers, and snacks, taking armfuls of items she does not need.
She drives to three supermarkets on coupon day and buys a year's supply of shampoo and detergent, then stacks the unopened bottles in a hallway closet to avoid paying regular price later.
He uses a grocery store self-checkout to ring expensive apples as the cheapest variety so the total comes out lower.
After receiving too much change from a distracted cashier, she notices the mistake and folds the extra bills into her wallet without saying anything.
A landlord applies automatic rent increases each year to a tenant in a deteriorating building and ignores repeated requests to repair ceiling leaks because the unit still brings in steady cash.
He rounds each roommate's share of utilities and internet up to the next dollar in the budgeting app and lets the small overages accumulate in his own payment account.
During a regional shortage, she buys most of the low-cost insulin available at several neighborhood pharmacies and reposts the boxes online the same day at a steep markup.
A restaurant owner changes the discard labels on seafood that has sat too long and serves it at full price rather than record the loss.
To avoid hiring extra staff, a day-care operator waters down prepared formula and stretches snack portions beyond the posted meal plan.
After their father dies, he locks the workshop and tells his brothers they can only retrieve tools that were informally understood to be theirs if they first pay him what he calls handling costs.
She buys blocks of tickets to a popular children's concert during presale and reposts them that afternoon at four times face value.
He collects cash from a newly arrived family for three beds in an unpermitted basement sleeping room and avoids written receipts so he can keep the rent off the books.
She submits the same taxi receipt to both her employer and the conference organizer and keeps both reimbursements when the payments arrive.
He keeps a drawer full of unused gift cards and refuses to spend them even on groceries he needs because he likes seeing the stored total remain unchanged.
A branch manager delays releasing earned commissions to junior agents until after quarter-end so the office appears to be holding more cash on its books.
He finds a smartphone in a gym locker room, removes the payment cards tucked inside the case, and turns in only the phone at the front desk.
She persuades her mother to sign forms granting access to a retirement account, then transfers money out in small amounts to buy luxury handbags before relatives notice.
He completes a community pantry's volunteer orientation only to qualify for the volunteers-only grocery line, then takes the maximum allotment each week without ever returning to help.
She keeps a jar of mixed coins on her dresser and walks to work in the rain rather than use any of it for bus fare because she does not want the total to go down.
